quiz.explanation: Li Si's decree had left official copies of all texts in the imperial library in Xianyang. When Xiang Yu burned the Qin palaces in 206 BCE, he destroyed those backup copies. This is why the Han spent decades reconstructing texts from fragmentary copies hidden by scholars and from the memories of elderly survivors. Works that had been completely suppressed during the Qin period could not be recovered at all.

image.caption: A later Chinese illustration depicting the Burning of Books, 213 BCE

nodes.narrative:
The Burning of Books was Li Si's idea, and it captured the contradictions of the man who had done more than anyone except Qin Shi Huang (Ying Zheng) to build the Qin state. Li Si was brilliant, pragmatic, and ultimately self-destructive. He had standardized Chinese writing — a genuinely revolutionary administrative achievement. He had argued against feudal enfeoffment and for centralized bureaucratic governance, a decision that proved correct across two thousand years of history. He was also capable of recommending the destruction of China's intellectual heritage to protect his own political position.

Li Si had arrived in Qin around 247 BCE from the state of Chu, where he had studied under the philosopher Xunzi alongside the Legalist theorist Han Feizi. He found a Qin court then dominated by Lü Buwei and positioned himself carefully, rising through the bureaucracy as Ying Zheng consolidated power. By the time of unification, he was the chancellor — the most powerful official in the empire — and the primary architect of the standardization programs.

His co-conspiracy with Zhao Gao to suppress the First Emperor's death and forge the succession decree was the beginning of his end. Li Si's motive was survival: he calculated that Fusu would remove him from power, while Huhai would be manageable. He was wrong. Within two years, Zhao Gao had outmaneuvered him completely. Zhao Gao arrested Li Si in 208 BCE on charges of treason, subjected him to torture, and extracted a false confession. Li Si was executed by the Five Punishments — mutilation followed by being cut in half at the waist — in the marketplace of Xianyang. The man who had built the empire's legal system was destroyed by it.

Li Si's trajectory from reformer to self-serving conspirator illustrates a structural weakness of the Qin state: it had no institutional mechanism for loyal opposition or principled dissent. The only politics available under Legalism was palace maneuvering. When palace maneuvering became the only game, it consumed the state's most capable administrators.

quiz.question: Li Si had studied philosophy under Xunzi alongside another famous thinker who developed Legalist theory. Who was that fellow student?
options: Mozi, whose utilitarian philosophy shared some Legalist premises
Mencius, the Confucian philosopher who debated Legalist ideas
Han Feizi, who synthesized and articulated Legalist philosophy more fully than anyone
Zhuangzi, the Daoist philosopher and contemporary of Shang Yang
quiz.explanation: Han Feizi and Li Si were both students of the Confucian philosopher Xunzi, who took a more pessimistic view of human nature than Mencius. Han Feizi synthesized the Legalist tradition into its most complete form, writing the Han Feizi — a text so impressive that when Qin Shi Huang read it, he reportedly said he would die without regret if he could only meet its author. Han Feizi came to Qin as a diplomat and was imprisoned, apparently at Li Si's instigation, and died in custody.

quiz.question: What was the method of Li Si's execution in 208 BCE?
options: Beheading in the palace courtyard, his body displayed at the city gate
Exile to the far south, where he died in transit
The Five Punishments — mutilation followed by being cut in half at the waist
Drowning in the Wei River, the traditional punishment for treason in Qin law
quiz.explanation: The Five Punishments (wuxing) was an extreme form of execution involving successive mutilations — tattooing, cutting off the nose, cutting off the feet — followed by flogging to death and, in Li Si's case, being cut in half at the waist. Zhao Gao chose the most brutal method available under Qin law for the man who had arguably designed that legal code. Li Si reportedly wept in prison and told his son about all the simple pleasures he would miss — he had no philosophical composure at the end.

nodes.narrative:
The man who destroyed the Qin dynasty was not a rebel general or a foreign conqueror. He was a palace eunuch who had been head of the imperial secretariat — the official who controlled the flow of documents to and from the emperor. Zhao Gao had served the First Emperor for decades, tutored the young Huhai, and understood better than anyone else how imperial information flows worked. When the emperor died at Shaqiu, Zhao Gao had everything he needed to reshape the succession.

His co-conspiracy with Li Si to place Huhai on the throne was calculated: he had tutored Huhai and understood his character. A devoted, pleasure-seeking young emperor would be easy to manage, while the capable Fusu would not be. After Huhai became Qin Er Shi, Zhao Gao systematically eliminated every official who might challenge his influence. Senior ministers were accused of crimes under Qin's harsh legal code and executed. The First Emperor's own sons and daughters — potential rallying points for opposition — were killed in purges.

Zhao Gao's most famous act was the deer-horse incident: he brought a deer before the Second Emperor and called it a horse. He watched which officials agreed and which corrected him. Those who said 'deer' were later arrested and executed on unrelated charges. The test eliminated the last courtiers willing to tell the emperor the truth. 'Pointing to a deer and calling it a horse' became one of the most durable idioms in the Chinese language — describing the use of deliberate falsehood as a political loyalty test.

Zhao Gao engineered Qin Er Shi's suicide in 207 BCE as rebel armies closed in, installing a third ruler, Ziying, who was intended to be another puppet. Ziying was not. He lured Zhao Gao to a meeting under pretense of illness and had him executed, along with his entire family. Forty-six days later, Ziying surrendered to Liu Bang's army at the gates of Xianyang. Zhao Gao, the man who had broken the dynasty from within, did not live to see its fall.

quiz.question: Zhao Gao's 'deer-horse' test is now one of China's most famous political idioms. What was its purpose?
options: To identify officials who were too literal-minded to serve in a flexible administration
To determine which officials would lie to support him and which would tell the emperor the truth — eliminating the latter
To test the Second Emperor's own perception and judgment
To determine which officials had been bribed by rebel factions outside the capital
quiz.explanation: The point was not the deer or the horse — it was identifying which officials would contradict Zhao Gao to tell the emperor the truth. Those who did were later killed. After the test, the emperor was surrounded only by men who would confirm whatever Zhao Gao said. The phrase 指鹿为马 (zhǐ lù wéi mǎ — 'pointing to a deer, calling it a horse') remains in everyday Chinese use today to describe deliberate gaslighting in political contexts.

quiz.question: How did the final Qin ruler Ziying manage to kill Zhao Gao, the powerful eunuch who had controlled the two previous emperors?
options: He poisoned Zhao Gao's tea during a private audience
He allied with rebel generals outside the capital who arrested Zhao Gao when he tried to negotiate
He feigned illness and lured Zhao Gao to a meeting, then had him seized and executed
He issued an edict to the imperial guard while Zhao Gao was away from the palace
quiz.explanation: Ziying refused to follow the pattern of his predecessors. He pretended to be ill and unable to receive Zhao Gao at the usual locations, then summoned Zhao Gao to his private chambers. When Zhao Gao arrived, Ziying's men seized and executed him, along with his entire family. Ziying then surrendered to Liu Bang 46 days later — having eliminated the man who destroyed the dynasty, but too late to save it.

nodes.narrative:
The spark that ended the Qin dynasty was a rainstorm. In the summer of 209 BCE, two constables named Chen Sheng and Wu Guang were leading 900 conscripted laborers to a garrison posting in the far north. Floods delayed them past the legal deadline. Under Qin law, arriving late to a military posting was punishable by death — the same punishment as rebellion.

Chen Sheng's logic, as ancient sources report it, was straightforward: 'Death faces us either way. If we are to die, why not die for something great?' He and Wu Guang killed their two supervising officers and raised a rebellion among their 900 conscripts. They moved quickly, exploiting the latent resentment of populations ground down by continuous conscription, heavy taxes, and the collective punishment provisions of Qin's legal code. Within weeks, Chen Sheng had declared himself king of a revived Chu state.

The speed at which the rebellion spread revealed how thin Qin's popular support actually was. A state held together by fear rather than loyalty could not endure once the fear broke down. Dozens of local leaders seized the moment — former nobles of the conquered states, ambitious peasants, local strongmen — and raised their own armies. Two of them would prove decisive: Xiang Yu, grandson of the last Chu general to die fighting Qin, and Liu Bang, a local constable from Chu who had freed his own conscript detail and become an outlaw.

Chen Sheng himself was killed by his own coachman within six months — another sign of how unstable the new order was. But his rebellion had already accomplished something irreversible: it demonstrated that Qin could be defied. Every rebel leader who followed — including the ones who eventually destroyed the dynasty and founded the Han — explicitly credited Chen Sheng with having shown that it was possible.

quiz.question: Under what specific provision of Qin law did Chen Sheng and Wu Guang calculate they faced death for simply being delayed by floods?
options: The law against abandoning your assigned garrison without a sealed imperial permit
The law making late arrival at a military posting punishable by death
The collective punishment provision that held all 900 conscripts responsible for any desertion
The law requiring conscripts to arrive with a full complement or face beheading
quiz.explanation: Qin law prescribed death for arriving late to a military posting — a zero-tolerance standard designed to maintain the empire's massive conscript system. The floods that delayed Chen Sheng's group were not a recognized legal excuse. His reasoning — 'we die either way, so why not die for something?' — turned a bureaucratic death sentence into a revolutionary act. The rebels also sent signals to former nobles of the conquered states, framing the uprising as restoration of the old kingdoms.

quiz.question: What was the fate of Chen Sheng, the leader who sparked the first major rebellion against the Qin?
options: He was captured by Qin forces and publicly executed in Xianyang as a warning to other rebels
He died in battle against Qin loyalist forces near the capital
He surrendered to Qin in exchange for a pardon and lived out his life as a minor official
He was killed by his own coachman within six months of starting the rebellion
quiz.explanation: Chen Sheng's rebellion collapsed almost as quickly as it spread. He declared himself king, but his leadership was chaotic and his allies unreliable. Within six months, he was killed by his own driver — a sign of the instability within the rebel coalition. But the rebellion had already demonstrated that Qin could be challenged. Liu Bang explicitly performed rituals honoring Chen Sheng after founding the Han, crediting him with having broken the psychological barrier to revolt.

nodes.narrative:
The Chen Sheng uprising of 209 BCE shattered Qin's aura of invincibility and set dozens of rebel armies in motion across the former six kingdoms. But Qin still had substantial military forces. Its main army — under the general Zhang Han, who had replenished his force partly with convict laborers from the mausoleum construction — had defeated several rebel armies and besieged the remnant Zhao forces at Julu. Several other rebel generals had arrived to relieve the siege and then camped nearby, watching. None dared attack Zhang Han's 300,000-strong force.

Xiang Yu changed the calculation. He had risen rapidly through the rebel coalition, partly through personal charisma and partly through the terrifying effectiveness of his personal fighting ability — ancient sources describe him as capable of lifting a bronze cauldron and legendary in close combat. When the Chu rebel council hesitated to let him lead a relief force to Julu, he killed the council leader and took command himself.

After crossing the Zhang River with his force, Xiang Yu ordered his soldiers to sink the boats, smash the cooking pots, and burn the camp shelters. Each soldier carried only three days' rations. There was no retreat possible. The other rebel generals, watching from their camps, saw Xiang Yu's men attack the Qin lines nine times with total ferocity — no reserve, no strategic fallback. Zhang Han's army was broken. He eventually surrendered with 200,000 remaining soldiers, whom Xiang Yu had executed by burying alive, fearing they would revolt.

Julu ended the Qin dynasty's military capacity. With Zhang Han's main army gone, there was no force left to defend Xianyang. The road to the capital was open, and both Xiang Yu and Liu Bang were racing toward it — beginning the rivalry that would produce the Han dynasty.

quiz.question: The phrase 'breaking the cauldrons and sinking the boats' (破釜沉舟) entered Chinese as an idiom for total commitment with no retreat. What were the three specific actions Xiang Yu ordered?
options: Sink the boats, smash the cooking pots, and burn the camp shelters
Sink the boats, bury the food stores, and swear an oath to die before retreating
Destroy the bridge behind them, smash the cooking pots, and divide all remaining gold among the troops
Burn the boats, scatter the horses, and seal the supply wagons so no one could access provisions
quiz.explanation: Xiang Yu ordered the boats sunk, the cooking pots (cauldrons) smashed, and the camp shelters burned — leaving each soldier with only three days of carried rations and no means of cooking, retreat, or camping. The psychological effect on his own troops was as important as the logistical one: with no retreat possible, fighting to the death was the only rational option. The phrase 破釜沉舟 is still used in Chinese today to mean committing to something with no fallback.

quiz.question: After defeating Zhang Han at Julu, Xiang Yu executed Zhang Han's 200,000 surrendered soldiers. What was his stated reason?
options: He wanted to prevent them from being re-enlisted by Qin loyalists in other parts of the empire
The rules of the rebel coalition required that all Qin soldiers who resisted be executed as a deterrent
He was avenging the Qin massacre at Changping, which had killed 400,000 Zhao soldiers
He feared that so many Qin soldiers, if released or incorporated, would revolt against his Chu forces
quiz.explanation: Xiang Yu's calculation was that he could neither feed 200,000 prisoners nor safely integrate them into his army. Released, they might regroup or defect. Incorporated, they might turn on his Chu soldiers who outnumbered them. He chose execution. The same logic Qin general Bai Qi had applied at Changping in 260 BCE — when 400,000 Zhao prisoners were executed — was now applied to Qin's own soldiers. Xiang Yu's decision turned many former Qin territories against him.

nodes.narrative:
The race to Xianyang in 206 BCE was also a contest in political style. Liu Bang's army arrived first, entering the capital peacefully. He sealed the palace storehouses without looting them, issued a proclamation promising to abolish Qin's harsh laws and replace them with three simple rules — no killing, no injuring, no stealing — and withdrew his troops from the city to camp outside the walls. The last Qin ruler, Ziying, surrendered within days. Liu Bang spared his life.

When Xiang Yu arrived weeks later with a far larger army, he made the opposite choices. He executed Ziying. He burned the Qin palace complex — fires that ancient sources say burned for three months. He looted the treasury and carted the wealth back to his own domain. He divided China among eighteen kings, restoring something close to the Zhou feudal order he had grown up being told was the proper way to govern. Liu Bang received the remote, poorly connected territory west of the mountains — a deliberate humiliation, given that Liu Bang had arrived first.

The contrast in the two men's approaches to Xianyang prefigured the four-year civil war that followed. Xiang Yu was the greater warrior — he never lost a major battle until the final campaign. But Liu Bang was the better politician. He kept his generals loyal with rewards and authority. He treated conquered populations leniently. He was willing to retreat strategically and fight again from a better position. At the Battle of Gaixia in 202 BCE, surrounded and outnumbered, Xiang Yu fought until the end and committed suicide at the Wujiang River, refusing to retreat across the river to fight another day.

Emperor Gaozu (Liu Bang) founded the Han dynasty. He immediately reversed Qin's most hated policies — reducing taxes, pardoning criminals, demobilizing armies — while keeping every administrative institution Qin had built. The Qin legacy, stripped of its brutality, proved remarkably durable. The Han ruled for four centuries, cementing the model for all subsequent Chinese dynasties. The fifteen years of Qin governance shaped China more profoundly than almost any other period of comparable length in world history.

quiz.question: What three-rule legal code did Liu Bang promise to replace Qin's complex harsh system with when he entered Xianyang?
options: Honor your officials, pay your taxes, do not resist the army
Obey the emperor, work your land, do not criticize the government
Respect the elderly, do not move without permission, pay fines rather than face imprisonment
No killing, no injuring, no stealing
quiz.explanation: Liu Bang's 'three-chapter covenant' (约法三章) — no killing, no injuring, no stealing — was brilliant political theater. It distilled Qin's immense and punishing legal code into the simplest possible promise: I will not hurt you. The contrast with Qin's system, which had executed people for arriving late, was stark and deliberate. The proclamation won enormous popular goodwill and directly contrasted with Xiang Yu's subsequent burning and looting of the city.

quiz.question: After his defeat, the warrior Xiang Yu found himself cornered near the Wujiang River with a small band of followers. A boatman offered to ferry him across to safety. What did Xiang Yu do?
options: He crossed the river, rallied new forces in Chu, and fought Liu Bang for another two years
He accepted the escape, but was captured when Liu Bang's forces crossed in pursuit
He refused to cross and committed suicide — saying he could not face the elders of his home region after his defeat
He surrendered to Liu Bang on condition that his followers be spared, then was executed
quiz.explanation: Xiang Yu refused to cross. His exact words, as recorded: 'Heaven has abandoned me — why should I cross? Even if the fathers and elders of Jiangdong took pity on me and made me king again, I could not face them.' He fought on foot until he had killed dozens more enemies, then committed suicide. Liu Bang reportedly wept and buried him with honor. The scene became one of Chinese literature's most enduring images of tragic heroism — a man who could win every battle and still lose the war.

image.caption: Map of the Chu-Han Contention (206-202 BCE) between Xiang Yu and Liu Bang following the Qin dynasty's collapse
